artifacts.content:
From: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@email.com>
To: Winter Park Traffic Safety Committee <safetycommittee@cityofwinterpark.org>
Date: 2026-02-11 08:17
Subject: Primary Contact Info for North Phelps Traffic-Calming Petition

Hi everyone,

Following up on our neighborhood meeting last Sunday, I’m sending over my details so you can list a single point of contact for the North Phelps Avenue traffic-calming petition.

Please list me as:

Primary contact: Laura Marie Henderson
Address: 742 North Phelps Avenue, Winter Park, FL 32789
Primary contact phone number: (407) 555-4829
Email: laura.henderson@email.com

A bit of context you can include in the petition narrative if it’s helpful:

My husband Dan and I have lived on North Phelps since 2017, and our kids, Ethan (10) and Chloe (7), walk and bike along this stretch daily to get to the bus stop near Phelps and Minnesota and to friends’ houses. Over the past couple of years we’ve seen traffic volume and speed increase noticeably, especially with cars cutting through from Minnesota and Mizell.

In early 2025, Ethan had a near-miss when a car rolling the stop sign off Minnesota braked hard as he stepped toward the crosswalk. Then in late October 2025, a delivery van clipped a parked car down the block. Neither incident resulted in injuries, thankfully, but they were clear warnings that the current conditions aren’t sustainable, especially with so many families on this street.

Our goal with this petition is to request a formal traffic study and explore options like speed humps, clearer crosswalk markings, and better signage at the Minnesota and Mizell intersections.

Feel free to call or text me at any time if you need more information or signatures.

Best,
Laura

metadata.subject: Primary Contact Info for North Phelps Traffic-Calming Petition

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
To: Michael Rivera <m.rivera736@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-11 08:14 AM
Subject: Contact info for the neighborhood traffic form

Hey Mike,

Thanks again for mentioning that city “Neighborhood Traffic Concern” form at the block get‑together. I finally pulled it up last night, and it’s asking for a primary contact phone number for the location. I got stuck for a second trying to decide what to put.

I’m leaning toward using my personal cell since I’m the one who’s been emailing the city already: 407‑213‑8644. I figure if they need to reach someone quickly about Phelps, it’ll probably be during the day when I’m toggling between Zoom calls anyway. I’d rather not list the AdventHealth office line because calls get lost in the main switchboard, and I definitely don’t want to put Dan’s work number on there without asking him.

I started to write a description of the speeding, but it made me realize I haven’t actually kept track of when or how often the speeding happens, it just feels like people are flying down Phelps whenever I happen to look out the front window. I might set up a quick notes doc and jot down times for a week so it doesn’t sound so hand‑wavy.

If you’re okay with it, I’ll list you as a secondary contact with your cell (the one you gave the Amazon driver the other day: 321‑555‑9032). Let me know if that’s still current.

Also, are you fine with us using 736 as an example address on the form, or do you think it should just be mine?

Laura

metadata.subject: Contact info for the neighborhood traffic form

From: Daniel Henderson <dan.henderson@civengconsult.com>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-15 07:02 PM
Subject: Use the house line for city follow-ups

Laura,

Just looked over the draft of that traffic-calming petition you left open on the laptop. For the primary contact phone number, I really think you should use the house line instead of your cell. If they call during dinner or after work, it’s easier for either of us to pick up.

The landline is still active: 407‑644‑3187. Spectrum tried to get us to cancel it last year, but honestly it’s useful for exactly this kind of thing. Plus, you’re always juggling AdventHealth calls on your mobile, and you don’t need city staff ringing you while you’re in the middle of your screening campaign meetings.

If they need a backup, you can add my cell, 321‑407‑1180, in the “secondary contact” section. Just don’t put my Maitland office number on there; it goes through the receptionist and I’ll never get the message.

Also, your description of the speeding is pretty emotional (understandably). You even wrote that you “haven’t actually kept track of when or how often the speeding happens,” which is honest, but maybe soften that so it doesn’t sound like we’re exaggerating. Maybe say we’ve observed frequent speeding during school commute times instead.

Let’s print a copy so I can mark it up before you submit.

Dan

artifacts.content:
From: HOA Board <board@lakewillowhoa.org>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-09 03:48 PM
Subject: Re: Drafting contact section for traffic petition

Hi Laura,

Thanks for volunteering to be the point person on the North Phelps traffic petition. In the “location information” section, the city just needs a reliable number where they can reach someone during normal business hours.

Given that you’re heading up the effort, it makes sense to list your preferred contact: the mobile number you included on your HOA profile last year, 321‑555‑7408. That’s what we have on file for you, and it’s the one we’ve used when we’ve needed a quick answer about block party permits, etc.

You had asked whether you should instead list the Henderson household landline. The city has told us in the past that a single, direct line to the primary organizer is best, rather than a shared family number. If you’d like, you can still add the home line and your husband’s cell in the “additional contacts” area, but the 321‑555‑7408 number should be fine as the primary.

Also, your draft narrative about the speeding concerns looks strong. Don’t worry too much about not having exact logs; most residents don’t. You mentioned you haven’t actually kept track of when or how often the speeding happens and that you mainly notice it when you look out your front window—that kind of day‑to‑day observation is exactly what they expect.

Let us know once you’ve submitted the form so we can share it with the rest of the association.

Best,
Lake Willow HOA Board

Title: Walk-through to verify house count for petition
Date: 2026-01-13 6:00 PM–7:00 PM
Location: North Phelps Ave (Minnesota to Mizell)
Attendees: Laura Henderson; Patricia Nguyen; Eleanor Brooks

Notes:

Goal: Do an in-person walk-through of North Phelps to confirm how many residential lots we should list as “affected properties” on the neighborhood traffic-calming petition.

Working assumptions going in:
- Patricia’s earlier spreadsheet listed **18 properties** based on the county parcel map.
- Laura’s quick walk on 1/9 estimated closer to **22 homes** that feel directly impacted.
- Eleanor thinks the city might want us to include the corner houses that technically face Minnesota, which would push the total closer to **“about twenty” plus a few corner lots**.

Tasks during walk-through:
1. Start at the Minnesota intersection and walk north, counting only parcels with driveways directly on North Phelps.
2. Mark duplexes and flag whether we count them as one or two lots.
3. Decide whether the corner properties (on Minnesota and on Mizell) belong in the “affected properties” total or in a separate “adjacent” category.
4. Take quick phone photos of any ambiguous parcels to ask the city planning staff later.

We’ll finalize a **ballpark property count** at the end of the walk and use that number in the draft petition before sending it to the city’s transportation office for review.

metadata.title: Walk-through to verify house count for petition

artifacts.content:
From: City of Winter Park Transportation <trafficcalming@cityofwinterpark.org>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-01-20 03:47 PM
Subject: RE: Pre-application questions – North Phelps Ave traffic petition

Dear Ms. Henderson,

Thank you for your interest in the City of Winter Park’s Neighborhood Traffic Management Program and for your proactive efforts on North Phelps Avenue.

In response to your question about how to report the number of affected properties/lots on the petition form:

1. For planning purposes, our office’s GIS layer currently identifies **19 residential parcels** fronting the section of North Phelps you described (between Minnesota and Mizell).
2. Corner parcels whose primary driveway access is on Minnesota or Mizell are typically categorized as “adjacent but not fronting” and are not automatically included in the “total affected properties” count. However, we encourage you to obtain signatures from those homeowners as well, as they may still experience cut-through traffic.
3. If you and your neighbors believe additional homes are directly impacted, you may indicate an **estimated range** (e.g., “approximately 18–20 properties”) in the comments section of the petition. Our staff will verify the final number during the field review.

Please note that for the petition to be considered valid, signatures are required from at least 60% of the identified affected properties. Using the current GIS estimate of **19 parcels**, this would mean signatures from a minimum of 12 households.

Feel free to follow up if you have further questions about how to complete the location information section of the petition.

Sincerely,

Karen Delgado
Transportation Planning Specialist
City of Winter Park

metadata.subject: RE: Pre-application questions – North Phelps Ave traffic petition

Right now we have 11 greens, which should put us just above the 60% threshold if the city sticks with the **19-parcel** baseline. Still, I’d feel better if we got at least 14 or 15 households on board.

Let me know if you spot any missing addresses or if I mis-numbered a duplex as one lot.

metadata.title: Walk-through to verify affected properties for Phelps petition
metadata.location: Corner of N Phelps Ave & Minnesota, Winter Park

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Collins <scollins@cityofwinterpark.org>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@email.com>
Date: 2026-01-22 10:47
Subject: RE: Clarification on "affected properties" definition

Hi Ms. Henderson,

Thanks again for coming to the Transportation Advisory Board meeting last night and for your thoughtful comments about conditions on North Phelps Avenue.

Per your question about how the City defines the “total number of affected properties/lots” for a traffic calming petition:

1. We typically look at the continuous street segment you’re concerned about—in this case, the block from Minnesota to Mizell.
2. We then count each parcel that has direct driveway access to that segment. Corner parcels are counted if their primary driveway is on the petition street.
3. Multi-family parcels (like duplexes) usually count as one “lot,” even if there are multiple units.

You mentioned in your email that your informal count came to **22 affected properties** along your block, including the two corner lots at each end. That number sounds reasonable as a starting point, though it is not the official figure we will use if you submit a petition. Once we receive your completed petition form, our engineering staff will run a parcel analysis in GIS and provide you with the official total.

I know you’re also tracking license plates and driver behavior after the recent near-miss involving your neighbor backing out of her driveway. While that documentation is not required for the petition, it can be helpful context when staff reviews the request.

Please let me know if you’d like to set up a short call to walk through the petition form line by line.

Best,
Sarah Collins
Transportation Planning Division
City of Winter Park

Quick gut-check before I finalize everything for the neighborhood traffic petition.

I’m planning to drop the signed packet at the City Winter Park (CWP) clerk’s office when I’m downtown for my AdventHealth meeting next **Thursday morning**. The clerk I spoke with yesterday said as long as it’s submitted **before March 1, 2026**, it will be placed on the same agenda as the other traffic-calming requests for our area. She emphasized the “date of submittal” is whatever day I physically hand it over at the counter, not the date on the signatures or the cover letter.

I’ve updated the cover letter to reference the near-miss with Ethan at the Minnesota crosswalk and Michael’s mess from last fall. In late October 2025, around 5:15 p.m., that delivery van sideswiped his parked Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps trying to get around a car that had stopped short. Now GEICO has jacked his premium up by about $75 a month and he’s still fighting with that body shop on Fairbanks over a $1,200 supplemental repair bill. I think it helps show there’s a pattern here, not just one paranoid mom.

Can you skim the draft letter tonight so I can print and attach it? If all looks good, I’ll aim to submit the petition to CWP on **February 28** when I’m already downtown.

Thanks,
Laura

Title: Tentative – Drop off neighborhood petition at CWP
Date: 2026-02-29 09:00–10:00
Location: City of Winter Park – City Hall, Clerk’s Office
Attendees: Laura Henderson

Notes:

Holding this as a tentative slot to submit the North Phelps traffic-calming petition to the City Winter Park (CWP) clerk. Need to confirm exact submission date based on when I can get final signatures from Patricia, Michael, and Eleanor.

Clerk on phone said: “We go by the date you submit it to CWP, not the date on your petition header, so just make sure you bring it in before the next agenda cut-off.” She mentioned that anything submitted on or **before February 29, 2026** should make it into the March agenda packet.

To-do before this appointment:
- Print final version of petition cover letter (include brief description of Ethan’s near-miss at Minnesota & Phelps).
- Add short paragraph about the late October 2025 incident when the delivery van sideswiped Michael Rivera’s Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps. Note how his GEICO premium went up about $75/month and he’s still arguing with the Fairbanks Ave. body shop over a $1,200 supplemental repair bill.
- Double-check addresses and house numbers for all signers.

If I can’t make this time, reschedule to another morning the week **before March 1** so the submittal date still falls within the clerk’s cutoff window.

metadata.location: City of Winter Park – City Hall, Clerk’s Office

artifacts.content:
From: City of Winter Park Clerk’s Office <clerk@cityofwinterpark.gov>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-20 15:03
Subject: RE: Neighborhood Petition – Submittal Procedures

Dear Ms. Henderson,

Thank you for your inquiry regarding the neighborhood traffic-calming petition for North Phelps Avenue.

To clarify your question about the “date of submittal of petition to CWP”: this is the calendar date on which our office receives your completed petition materials, either in person at the clerk’s counter or via mail. Based on our conversation, you indicated you plan to deliver the documents in person **during the last week of February 2026**. Any petition received on or before **February 29, 2026** will be eligible for inclusion in the March Transportation Advisory Board agenda packet, assuming all required components are present.

Please ensure your packet includes:
1. Completed petition form with signatures.
2. Cover letter summarizing your concerns (you mentioned a near-miss involving your son and a separate incident in late October 2025 where a delivery van sideswiped your neighbor’s parked vehicle, resulting in increased insurance premiums and additional repair costs).
3. A simple sketch or map of the affected block.

When you arrive, please check in at the main reception desk and ask for the clerk on duty. We will stamp your petition with the official **received date** at that time.

Sincerely,

Angela Ruiz
Deputy City Clerk
City of Winter Park

metadata.sender: City of Winter Park Clerk’s Office <clerk@cityofwinterpark.gov>
metadata.subject: RE: Neighborhood Petition – Submittal Procedures

artifacts.content:
Title: Reschedule – CWP petition drop-off
Date: 2026-03-01 11:30–12:15
Location: Winter Park City Hall
Attendees: Laura Henderson

Description:

Had to move the original petition drop-off because Ethan’s soccer scrimmage got shifted. This new slot is the **day before** the date I actually plan to submit the petition to the City Winter Park (CWP) clerk. Using this event as a reminder to:

- Reprint the petition (kids spilled juice on the last set of signatures).
- Re-read the cover letter, especially the section about the late October 2025 delivery van that sideswiped Michael’s Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps and how his GEICO premium jumped by about $75/month, plus the lingering $1,200 body shop dispute on Fairbanks.
- Double-check that the header lists “North Phelps Avenue – traffic calming request” and that the signature dates are all from January and February 2026.

Actual submittal date to CWP clerk will be **after this reminder**, when I can go on my lunch break from AdventHealth. I need to make sure the petition is turned in **sometime in the first week of March**, but this calendar entry is just to prep everything and not the official date the city will stamp on the petition.

From: Patricia Nguyen <pnguyen751@example.com>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-27 21:18
Subject: Petition – did it get turned in?

Hey Laura,

Just checking in before I crash for the night – did you ever make it to City Hall today, or are you still planning to submit the petition to CWP **next week**?

When we were walking back from ice cream, you mentioned maybe dropping it off on **Friday, February 27**, since you’d already be halfway downtown for that AdventHealth meeting. If you didn’t get to it, no worries, I’m just curious what date will end up being the “official” submittal date they stamp on the petition. I want to be able to tell the neighbors roughly when the clock starts for the city’s response.

I’m really glad you included Michael’s situation in the cover letter. That late October 2025 mess with the delivery van sideswiping his Subaru in front of 746 North Phelps is exactly the kind of thing the city needs to hear about. The fact that GEICO raised his premiums by about $75 a month and he’s still arguing with that body shop on Fairbanks over a $1,200 extra repair bill really shows how the speeding and tight clearance on our block have real financial consequences.

Anyway, let me know if the petition went in today or if you’re aiming for a different date early in March.

– Patricia

metadata.subject: Petition – did it get turned in?

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
To: neighborhoodsafety@cityofwinterpark.org
Date: 2026-02-22 09:14 AM
Subject: Clarification on petition timeline for North Phelps traffic concerns

Hi City of Winter Park team,

Thank you again for speaking with me during last week’s virtual info session about residential traffic calming. I’m starting to pull together the formal petition for North Phelps Avenue (between Minnesota and Mizell), and I want to be sure I understand the timing correctly.

You mentioned that once I have signatures from at least 65% of the affected households, I can email a scanned copy of the petition to this address and then drop off the original at City Hall. I wrote down that the “date of submittal” for the petition will be whatever day your office stamps it as received, whether that’s the day I email it or the day I bring the hard copy in, whichever happens first.

Can you confirm that if I turn in the signatures sometime the week of March 9, 2026, the stamped submittal date would be that same business day? I’m trying to coordinate this with my work schedule at AdventHealth and a rescheduled follow-up at the Winter Park Women’s Health Center. On January 9, 2026, I missed a similar appointment because I was stuck in our driveway at 742 North Phelps while cut-through traffic blocked me in, which ended up costing me a $50 no-show fee.

Once I know how the submittal date is set, I’ll finalize my plan for collecting the last few signatures from neighbors.

Best,
Laura Henderson
742 N. Phelps Ave

metadata.subject: Clarification on petition timeline for North Phelps traffic concerns

artifacts.content:
Title: Drop off traffic petition at City Hall
Date: 2026-03-11 12:00 PM–1:00 PM
Location: Winter Park City Hall – Engineering/Transportation Desk
Attendees: Laura Henderson

Description:
Tentative block to deliver original signed petition regarding speeding and cut-through traffic on North Phelps Ave (between Minnesota and Mizell) to the City of Winter Park (CWP) Transportation office.

Plan:
- Leave AdventHealth Orlando campus by 11:15 a.m. after morning status huddle.
- Park in visitor lot and be at Engineering/Transportation desk by noon.
- Ask staff to time-stamp the petition and confirm what they will record as the official “date of submittal of petition to CWP” for their internal tracking.
- Clarify whether the date on the neighborhood signatures matters if the petition is dropped off several days after the last signature is collected.

Context notes:
Because of the way cars whip around the curve between Minnesota and Mizell, Chloe (7) has been too scared to walk or bike to the bus stop since the September 18, 2025 incident when a white pickup nearly hit her and Patricia Nguyen’s daughter as they stepped out from between parked cars. Since then, I’ve been leaving work at AdventHealth early on Tuesdays and Thursdays at 3:30 p.m. to walk them to the corner myself, which already led to a written warning from my manager, Karen Lewis, about my “unreliable availability for late-afternoon client calls.”

Reminder: Bring printed photos of the bus stop area and notes from Ethan’s school counselor about his anxiety for additional context if they ask.

metadata.title: Drop off traffic petition at City Hall
metadata.location: Winter Park City Hall – Engineering/Transportation Desk

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Lewis <karen.lewis@adventhealth.com>
To: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@adventhealth.com>
Date: 2026-02-28 03:06 PM
Subject: Re: Requesting PTO to submit neighborhood petition

Hi Laura,

I’ve reviewed your PTO request for the afternoon of March 5, 2026, to take care of the traffic petition you’re organizing for North Phelps Avenue.

I can approve the request as long as you’re able to move the client status call currently set for 3:30 p.m. that day. I understand this petition is important to you and your family, especially after the November 6, 2025 incident you described, when a black SUV ran the stop sign near your driveway and nearly hit Ethan on his way to the Lakemont Elementary bus stop.

Please remember that, from HR’s perspective, the exact date you submit the petition to the City of Winter Park (CWP) is outside our purview. Whether you turn it in on March 5, later that week, or sometime the following week, what matters for our planning is just that your time away from AdventHealth Orlando is scheduled and covered.

Once you have a firm appointment or know which day you’ll actually take the petition to City Hall, update the PTO request with that date in the notes so I have it documented. Also, keep in mind that repeated early departures, like when you’ve had to leave at 3:30 p.m. on Tuesdays and Thursdays to walk Chloe to the bus stop, are still being monitored.

Thanks,
Karen

Karen Lewis
Marketing Director, AdventHealth Orlando

metadata.subject: Re: Requesting PTO to submit neighborhood petition

artifacts.content:
Title: Neighborhood petition planning meeting
Date: 2026-02-19 07:00 PM–08:30 PM
Location: Hendersons’ living room – 742 N. Phelps Ave
Attendees: Laura & Dan Henderson, Michael Rivera, Patricia Nguyen, Eleanor Brooks

Description:
Informal planning session to finalize details of the traffic safety petition before it is submitted to the City of Winter Park (CWP).

Agenda:
- Review draft language on speeding, rolling stops at Minnesota and Mizell, and cut-through traffic.
- Decide how many signatures we need from the houses between Minnesota and Mizell.
- Discuss timing: aim to finish collecting signatures by early March and then pick a specific business day to submit the petition at City Hall.
- Confirm who will actually bring the petition in and whether we should email a scanned copy first or just rely on the stamped hard copy for the official “date of submittal.”

Notes for discussion:
Laura to recap the November 6, 2025 near-miss when a black SUV failed to stop at the Minnesota Avenue sign and came within a few feet of hitting Ethan as he walked from their driveway to the Lakemont bus stop. Also mention the January 9, 2026 morning when she missed a follow-up appointment at the Winter Park Women’s Health Center because a line of cars using North Phelps as a cut-through blocked her driveway and caused a $50 no-show fee.

Action item: By the end of the meeting, agree on a target week in March for turning in the petition, but the exact submittal date will depend on everyone’s schedules.

From: Laura Henderson <laura.henderson@gmail.com>
To: Michael Rivera <m.rivera736@gmail.com>; Patricia Nguyen <pnguyen751@gmail.com>; Eleanor Brooks <eleanor729@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-03-07 08:42 PM
Subject: Petition status + when should I actually turn it in?

Hey everyone,

Quick update on the traffic petition: I’ve counted 24 signatures so far, which should put us over the threshold the City of Winter Park mentioned for our stretch of North Phelps between Minnesota and Mizell. Thank you all for going door to door this week.

I’m trying to decide which day to actually submit the petition to CWP. The staffer I spoke with said the official “date of submittal” will just be the day they receive and stamp it, whether I email a scan first or walk the original into City Hall.

My current thought is to email the scanned petition either Wednesday, March 11 or Thursday, March 12, then drop off the hard copy on my lunch break one of those days. That way, if something comes up with work or the kids, we’re still covered by at least having the electronic version in on time.

Given everything that’s happened—the November 6, 2025 near-miss with Ethan when the black SUV blew through the stop sign, plus Chloe still being afraid to walk or bike to the bus stop after that white pickup scared her on September 18, 2025—I don’t want to drag this out any longer than we have to.

Let me know if you see any issues with aiming for that mid-March submission window.

Laura

metadata.recipient: Michael Rivera <m.rivera736@gmail.com>; Patricia Nguyen <pnguyen751@gmail.com>; Eleanor Brooks <eleanor729@gmail.com>
metadata.subject: Petition status + when should I actually turn it in?
